Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Chapter 1 — Foundational Public Order Offences
This chapter covers the three highest-volume Public Order offences: intoxication in a public place (s.4),
disorderly conduct (s.5), and threatening / abusive / insulting behaviour (s.6). These are the everyday offences
of street policing. They are summary-only, fine-end, and frequently encountered alone or in combination.

1.1 Intoxication in a Public Place — s.4 CJ(PO)A 1994
Definition

Legal Framework
Statute: s.4 Criminal Justice (Public Order) Act 1994.
Elements: (a) accused is in a public place; (b) accused is intoxicated; (c) intoxication is to a degree giving rise
to reasonable apprehension of endangerment to self or others.
Public place: Includes any highway, road, street, bridge, footway, footpath, court, alley, passage; any place to
which the public have access whether as of right or by permission; licensed premises and the immediate
environs.
Intoxicated: Defined in s.3 — means under the intoxicating effect of alcohol, drugs, solvent, or any
combination.
Reasonable apprehension: Objective threshold — a reasonable Garda would apprehend possible
endangerment in the circumstances.
Court: Summary only — District Court.
Penalty: Class E fine.
Power of arrest: s.24 — a member may arrest without warrant any person whom the member finds committing
the offence.
Alternative disposal: Fixed Charge Notice under s.7 CJA 2003 / 2006 schemes — may be applicable; check
live FCN scheme.

Track A — Live Incident Response
Approach — single member generally sufficient; calm approach reduces escalation risk.
Assess — visible intoxication signs (speech, gait, smell of alcohol, presence of bottles, glassy eyes);
endangerment indicators (proximity to traffic, water, stairs, road; risk to others).
Engage — verbal first; “Where are you going … are you with someone … can someone collect you”.
Options — (a) no further action if person can be safely got home / to safer location; (b) FCN if available; (c)
arrest for s.4 if circumstances require.
Avoid — the temptation to arrest for sake of it where a verbal direction and welfare-side intervention suffices.

Track B — Post-Arrest Processing
Custody — medical assessment if intoxication severe; particular attention to combination intoxication (alcohol
+ drugs).
Detention regime — s.4 CJ(PO)A 1994 detention is brief; person is held until fit to be released safely.
Charge sheet or summons — charge sheet preferred where person identifiable and can be released on bail with
court date.
Where person not fit for interview — do not interview; await sobering, medical clearance.

Suspect Strategy and Arrest Power
Specimen Arrest Words
“[Name], I am arresting you for an offence under section 4 of the Criminal Justice (Public Order) Act 1994.
You are intoxicated in a public place to such an extent as gives rise to reasonable apprehension that you might
endanger yourself or another. You are not obliged to say anything unless you wish to do so but anything you

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

do say will be taken down in writing and may be given in evidence.”
Notebook
Time, place, person.
Visible indicators of intoxication — specific observations.
Endangerment factors — traffic, stairs, water, behaviour.
Verbal interventions before arrest.

Interview Plan
Interview is generally not required for s.4 — the offence is self-evident on the member’s observations. Where
interview is conducted (e.g., to clarify identity, address), use simple PEACE structure. Do not interview an
intoxicated person to obtain admissions — admissibility would be challenged on voluntariness (Madden).

PULSE / PEMS / File Index
PULSE — incident type Public Order, sub-type s.4 intoxication.
Notebook entry.
BWV clip (where activated).
Custody record.
Charge sheet.

Charge Selection
Scenario

Charge

Lone intoxicated person endangering
self

s.4 CJ(PO)A 1994

Intoxication + disorderly behaviour

s.4 + s.5 — both available, charge per circumstances

Intoxication + threatening behaviour

s.6 may be the better charge if threats are the dominant feature

Intoxication + obstruction of Garda

s.4 + s.19(1) CJ(PO)A 1994 obstruction

Specimen Wording — Notebook
“At 23:50 hrs on [date] at [location], a public place, I observed [name] who appeared to be heavily intoxicated.
He was unsteady on his feet, slurring his speech and stepping into the roadway in front of moving vehicles. I
formed the view that he was intoxicated to such an extent as to give rise to a reasonable apprehension that he
might endanger himself or others. I introduced myself, identified him by ID card, and at [time] I arrested him
for an offence contrary to s.4 CJ(PO)A 1994. I administered the caution: ‘You are not obliged to say
anything … ’. He replied: ‘[reply]’.”

Defence Will Attack
Not a public place — location was private / not within s.3 definition. Rebut with detailed location description;
reference to map / photograph.
Not intoxicated — medical condition mimicking intoxication (stroke, diabetic episode, head injury). Rebut by
medical assessment at custody; member observation thorough on suspect conduct.
Intoxication not at endangerment threshold — ordinary drunkenness. Rebut by specific endangerment
indicators — stepping in traffic, near water, on stairs.

Red Flag Escalation
Sudden change in presentation — collapse, loss of consciousness — medical emergency, ambulance.
Possible medical cause — head injury, diabetic, stroke, overdose — medical not custody.
Vulnerable adult — mental health, intoxication interaction, AMHS referral if appropriate.
Repeated arrests of same individual — welfare-side engagement, social services.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Common Pitfalls
Arresting where verbal direction and a lift home would suffice — disproportionate response.
Missing medical cause of presentation — head injury / diabetic / stroke.
Not documenting endangerment threshold — “intoxicated” alone is not the offence; the apprehension of
endangerment is the threshold.
Conducting an interview during intoxication — voluntariness challenge.

Ethics Touchpoints
Standard 4 — force minimised; verbal direction first.
Standard 6 — impartial response — same intervention regardless of demographic.
Welfare-side — vulnerable IP routed to medical / family where appropriate, not custody.

Compliment Points / Cross-References
Compliment-worthy where Garda chose verbal direction and family contact over arrest — the right call for
welfare and public confidence.
Cross-reference: Vol 1 (where intoxication crossed into property damage), Vol 2 (where intoxication crossed
into assault).

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

1.2 Disorderly Conduct in a Public Place — s.5 CJ(PO)A 1994
Definition

Legal Framework
Statute: s.5 Criminal Justice (Public Order) Act 1994.
Elements: (a) public place; (b) offensive conduct; (c) either (i) between midnight and 7 am, OR (ii) after a
request from a Garda to desist (at any time).
Offensive conduct: Unreasonable behaviour which, having regard to all the circumstances, is likely to cause
serious offence or serious annoyance to any person who is, or might reasonably be expected to be, aware of
such behaviour.
Public place: As per s.3.
Court: Summary only.
Penalty: Class D fine.
Power of arrest: s.24 — finds committing.
Fixed Charge Notice: Available under FCN scheme.

Track A — Live Incident Response
Verbal request to desist — the request itself is the trigger of the offence outside the midnight-to-7 am window.
Make the request clearly, identify yourself, give the person opportunity to comply.
Observe continued offensive conduct — the conduct after the request is what constitutes the offence.
Document the request — time, exact words, suspect response.
BWV on — captures the request, the response, the continued conduct.

Track B — Post-Arrest Processing
Custody, charge sheet or summons.
Brief processing — most s.5 matters are District Court summary.

Suspect Strategy and Arrest Power
Specimen Arrest Words
“[Name], I am arresting you for an offence under section 5 of the Criminal Justice (Public Order) Act 1994.
You have engaged in disorderly conduct in a public place after I requested you to desist. You are not obliged
to say anything unless you wish to do so but anything you do say will be taken down in writing and may be
given in evidence.”
Notebook Critical Entries
Time of original observation of offensive conduct.
Time and exact words of request to desist.
Person’s response — verbal and behavioural.
Continued conduct after request.
Time of arrest.

Interview Plan
Interview typically brief. Cover: identification; the conduct; awareness of the Garda request; reason for noncompliance. Most s.5 cases plead at District Court without contested interview.

PULSE / File Index
PULSE incident — Public Order, s.5 disorderly conduct.
Notebook.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

BWV clip(s).
Charge sheet.

Charge Selection
Scenario

Charge

Loud / offensive conduct after midnight, no Garda
request

s.5 (midnight–7am limb)

Loud / offensive conduct at any time, persists after
Garda request

s.5 (request limb)

Conduct also threatening

s.6 may be the better charge

Conduct involves intoxication

s.4 + s.5 — both available

Specimen Wording — Notebook
“At 02:15 hrs on [date] at [location], a public place, I observed [name] shouting loudly, kicking parked vehicles
and using obscene language at passers-by. I formed the view that this constituted offensive conduct likely to
cause serious annoyance to persons in the vicinity. I approached, identified myself, and at 02:16 hrs requested
him to desist. He replied: ‘F off’ and continued shouting and gesturing. At 02:17 hrs I arrested him for an
offence contrary to s.5 CJ(PO)A 1994 and administered the caution.”

Defence Will Attack
Not offensive conduct — ordinary noise / behaviour. Rebut with specific descriptions, BWV, witness
statements.
Outside public place — location was private. Rebut with location description, photographs.
Request not made / not understood — inadequate communication. Rebut with clear notebook entry of request
words, BWV capture.
Request not given reasonable opportunity to comply — arrest too soon after request. Rebut by documenting the
time between request and continued conduct.

Red Flag Escalation
Conduct escalating from disorderly to violent — charge ladder up to s.6 / s.16.
Group dynamics — multiple persons engaged — consider s.16 affray threshold.

Common Pitfalls
Failing to document the desist request adequately.
Arresting in the immediate aftermath of the request without giving opportunity to comply.
Charging s.5 where s.6 fits better (threats present).
Multiple s.5s on a single arrest where evidence sustains only one.

Ethics Touchpoints
Standard 6 — impartial application; same threshold across demographic.
De-escalation — the request to desist is itself an intervention; verbal first.

Compliment Points / Cross-References
Compliment-worthy where Garda effectively de-escalated by clear, professional request and the situation
resolved without arrest.
Cross-reference: s.6 (where threats present); s.16 (group affray).

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

1.3 Threatening, Abusive or Insulting Behaviour — s.6 CJ(PO)A 1994
Definition

Legal Framework
Statute: s.6 Criminal Justice (Public Order) Act 1994.
Elements: (a) public place; (b) threatening, abusive or insulting words or behaviour; (c) intent to provoke
breach of peace OR recklessness as to whether breach of peace occasioned.
Threatening, abusive, insulting: Ordinary meaning; assessed objectively in the context.
Breach of peace: Conduct likely to cause harm to person or property, or to put a person in fear of such harm.
Mens rea: Intent OR recklessness (subjective Murray standard).
Court: Summary only.
Penalty: 3 months / Class C fine.
Power of arrest: s.24 — finds committing.

Track A — Live Incident Response
Document the words / behaviour — BWV captures language; notebook records exact words.
Identify the audience — who heard, who was the target.
Assess breach-of-peace risk — was a breach actually occasioned, or was one imminent.
De-escalate where possible — the offence does not require arrest; verbal direction may suffice.
Where threshold met and offence ongoing, arrest.

Track B — Post-Arrest Processing
Custody, charge sheet or summons.
CCTV recovery from surrounding premises (capture of incident, audience reactions).
Witness statements — target IP if any, third-party witnesses.

Suspect Strategy and Arrest Power
Specimen Arrest Words
“[Name], I am arresting you for an offence under section 6 of the Criminal Justice (Public Order) Act 1994.
You have used threatening / abusive / insulting words or behaviour in a public place with intent to provoke a
breach of the peace [or being reckless as to whether a breach of the peace may be occasioned]. You are not
obliged to say anything unless you wish to do so but anything you do say will be taken down in writing and
may be given in evidence.”
Notebook
Exact words used — verbatim.
Behaviour observed — specific descriptions.
Audience / target.
Audience reaction — evidence of breach-of-peace risk.
Verbal interventions before arrest.

Interview Plan
Cover: identification; the words used (acceptance / denial); intent / awareness of audience; awareness that
conduct could provoke breach of peace; awareness of public place character. Where defence is mistaken
identity, focus interview on suspect’s presence and movement.

PULSE / File Index
PULSE incident — Public Order, s.6.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Notebook entries (member and any colleague witness).
BWV clips.
CCTV from surrounding premises.
IP and third-party witness statements.
Charge sheet.

Charge Selection
Scenario

Best Fit

Single-event threatening words at one IP

s.6

Targeted single threat to kill / cause serious harm

s.5 NFOAP threats (more serious; Vol 2)

Repeated pattern of contact

s.10 NFOAP harassment / s.39 2023 stalking (Vol 2)

Threats in domestic context

DV Act 2018 + s.5 / s.6 NFOAP

Conduct also involves assault / contact

s.2 / s.3 NFOAP assault + s.6 may both stand

Specimen Wording — Statement of Member
“At 22:50 hrs on [date] at [location], a public place where there were approximately 15 members of the public
present, I observed [name] shouting at [IP]. He used the words: ‘[verbatim]’ and made threatening hand gestures
towards [IP]. [IP] backed away visibly distressed. A small crowd of bystanders gathered. I formed the view that
the words and behaviour were threatening / abusive and were intended to provoke a breach of the peace or that
[name] was reckless as to whether a breach of the peace would be occasioned. At 22:51 hrs I arrested [name]
for an offence contrary to s.6 CJ(PO)A 1994 and administered the caution.”

Defence Will Attack
Not threatening / abusive / insulting — ordinary language in a robust exchange. Rebut by verbatim record,
audience reaction, context.
No intent or recklessness as to breach of peace — private joke / no audience. Rebut by audience present, target
visibly distressed.
Not public place — inside private premises. Rebut by location description.
Casey / identification — mistaken identity. Rebut by direct observation, BWV ID, CCTV, member proximity.

Red Flag Escalation
Threat content meets s.5 NFOAP threshold (kill / cause serious harm) — escalate to s.5 NFOAP threats.
Pattern over time — s.10 NFOAP harassment / s.39 stalking.
DV context — DV Act 2018 framework engages.
Hate-aggravated content (racial, religious, ethnic, etc.) — record explicitly; aggravating feature at sentencing;
consider relevant statutory provisions.

Common Pitfalls
Charging s.6 where the threat content actually meets s.5 NFOAP (more serious provision).
Missing the recklessness limb — a person who creates a row in public without specific intent may still be
reckless as to breach of peace.
Not documenting audience presence — the breach-of-peace dimension requires audience reaction or risk.
Verbatim language not captured — the exact words matter; “abusive language” is not sufficient evidential
detail.

Ethics Touchpoints
Standard 6 — impartial application across demographics, including where the abuse is directed at the Garda.
Standard 4 — measured response; verbal de-escalation preferred where threshold not met.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Hate-aggravated content — explicit recording supports victim and reflects organisational values.

Compliment Points / Cross-References
Compliment-worthy where Garda secured strong evidence (verbatim, BWV, witness statements) and the file
led to a clean prosecution.
Cross-reference: s.5 NFOAP threats (Vol 2); s.10 / s.39 harassment / stalking (Vol 2); DV Act 2018 (Vol 4);
hate aggravation framework.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Chapter 2 — Direction Offences
This chapter covers the offences premised on a Garda direction — the move-on power (s.8) and the wilful
obstruction of a Garda (s.9). These provisions give operational teeth to the dynamic-incident framework: they
allow a Garda to require a person to move from a location where their continued presence is causing or likely
to cause a problem, and they criminalise wilful interference with Garda duty.

2.1 Failure to Comply with Direction of Garda — s.8 CJ(PO)A 1994
Definition

Legal Framework
Statute: s.8 Criminal Justice (Public Order) Act 1994.
Two-stage structure: (i) Garda gives a lawful direction under s.8; (ii) person fails to comply.
Grounds for direction: Reasonable suspicion that person is acting in a manner giving rise to reasonable
apprehension for safety / peace, OR committing an offence under s.4, 5, 6, 7, or 8.
Direction: May be to desist, or to leave the vicinity, or both.
Court: Summary only.
Penalty: 6 months / Class D fine.
Power of arrest: s.24 — finds committing.

Track A — Live Incident Response
Form the lawful grounds first — reasonable suspicion based on observed conduct and circumstances.
Give a clear, specific direction — “Move on from this area” / “Desist from [conduct]” / “Leave this vicinity
now”.
Identify yourself as a member of An Garda Síochána (typically in uniform but verbal identification supports
clarity).
Allow reasonable opportunity to comply — a few moments, not a fraction of a second.
Observe whether the person complies.
Where non-compliance is wilful / sustained, arrest under s.24.

Track B — Post-Arrest Processing
Custody.
Charge sheet or summons.
Notebook entries critical — the direction itself must be evidenced precisely.
BWV capture if activated.

Suspect Strategy and Arrest Power
Specimen Direction Words
“[Name / individual], I am a member of An Garda Síochána. I am directing you under section 8 of the Public
Order Act to [desist from this behaviour / leave this area immediately]. If you fail to comply, you may be
arrested.”
Specimen Arrest Words
“[Name], I am arresting you for an offence under section 8 of the Criminal Justice (Public Order) Act 1994.
You have failed to comply with a direction I gave you under section 8 to [leave the area / desist]. You are not
obliged to say anything unless you wish to do so but anything you do say will be taken down in writing and
may be given in evidence.”
Notebook — Critical Entries

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Time the direction was given.
The grounds for the direction — what conduct, what reasonable suspicion, what reasonable apprehension.
Exact words of the direction.
Person’s acknowledgment / response.
Reasonable opportunity to comply — time interval.
Continued non-compliance — specific conduct.
Time of arrest.

Interview Plan
Cover: identification; understanding of the direction; reason for non-compliance; understanding of Garda
authority. Most s.8 cases are summary; interview often brief.

PULSE / File Index
PULSE incident — Public Order, s.8.
Notebook — detailed direction wording and grounds.
BWV clip.
Charge sheet.

Charge Selection
Scenario

Charge

Person refuses to move from area after lawful
direction

s.8

Person disrupts Garda duty actively (more than
failure to move)

s.19(1) obstruction

Person physically resists / threatens

s.19(3) assault on peace officer (Vol 2)

Specimen Wording — Statement of Member
“At 03:00 hrs on [date] at [location], a public place, I observed a group of approximately 8 persons including
[name] congregating at the entrance to [premises]. There had been a series of fights at the premises that night.
The group was vocal, intoxicated, and partly blocking access. I reasonably apprehended that the group’s
continued presence would lead to further disorder. At 03:01 hrs I addressed [name] directly: ‘I am a member
of An Garda Síochána. I am directing you under section 8 of the Public Order Act to leave this area immediately.
If you fail to comply you may be arrested.’ [Name] replied: ‘No, I’m staying’ and remained in place, continuing
to gesture at the door staff. At 03:02 hrs I formed the view that [name] had wilfully failed to comply and arrested
him under s.24 CJ(PO)A 1994 for an offence under s.8.”

Defence Will Attack
Direction not lawful — grounds not made out. Rebut with specific notebook entries documenting the conduct
and reasonable apprehension.
Direction not clear / not heard / not understood. Rebut by BWV, notebook of exact words, witness.
Insufficient opportunity to comply. Rebut by documented interval between direction and arrest.
Casey / identity — mistaken identity. Rebut by member observation, BWV, custody process.

Red Flag Escalation
Group dynamics — if other group members also non-compliant, possible affray / violent disorder threshold.
Active resistance to direction — s.19(1) obstruction (more serious).
Physical / threatening response — s.19(3) assault on peace officer.

Common Pitfalls

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Issuing a s.8 direction without forming or documenting the lawful grounds first — the direction must be founded
on reasonable suspicion / apprehension.
Not documenting the exact words of the direction.
Arresting immediately on “no” without giving a reasonable opportunity — a few moments matter.
Confusing s.8 (failure to comply with direction) with s.19(1) (active obstruction).

Ethics Touchpoints
Standard 4 — direction is itself a use of authority; should be exercised with restraint.
Standard 6 — same threshold across populations.
De-escalation — the direction is the de-escalation tool; arrest only on continued non-compliance.

Compliment Points / Cross-References
Compliment-worthy where s.8 direction successfully dispersed a developing situation without arrest — the
right use of the power.
Cross-reference: s.5 / s.6 (where ongoing offence); s.19(1) (where active obstruction); s.19(3) (where assault).

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

2.2 Wilful Obstruction — s.9 CJ(PO)A 1994
Definition
Note: s.9 is the public-passage obstruction offence. Distinguish from s.19(1) which is obstruction of a Garda in
execution of duty (Vol 2).

Legal Framework
Statute: s.9 Criminal Justice (Public Order) Act 1994.
Elements: (a) without lawful authority or reasonable excuse; (b) wilfully; (c) prevents or interrupts free
passage; (d) of person or vehicle; (e) in public place.
Wilful: Conscious, intentional act — not accidental obstruction.
Lawful authority: E.g., contractor with road closure permit, Garda direction itself.
Reasonable excuse: Limited; depends on circumstances (medical emergency, fall, etc.).
Court: Summary only.
Penalty: Class E fine.

Track A — Live Incident Response
Identify the obstruction — person standing in road, blocking entrance, lying down in protest, etc.
Form the view of wilful conduct (not accident, not medical episode).
Verbal direction to move — the s.8 direction power may overlap; consider which is the right path.
Document grounds, direction, response.
Arrest where threshold met and verbal direction insufficient.

Track B — Post-Arrest Processing
Custody, charge sheet or summons.
Notebook detail — the obstruction itself and its impact on passage.
BWV clip.
Witness statements where third parties affected by the obstruction (motorists held up, pedestrians blocked).

Suspect Strategy and Arrest Power
Specimen Arrest Words
“[Name], I am arresting you for an offence under section 9 of the Criminal Justice (Public Order) Act 1994.
You have, without lawful authority or reasonable excuse, wilfully prevented / interrupted the free passage of
persons / vehicles in this public place. You are not obliged to say anything unless you wish to do so but anything
you do say will be taken down in writing and may be given in evidence.”

Interview Plan
Cover: identification; reason for the conduct; awareness of effect on others; any claimed lawful authority or
reasonable excuse.

Charge Selection
Distinguish s.9 (obstruction of public passage by anyone) from s.19(1) CJ(PO)A 1994 (obstruction of a Garda
in execution of duty). They are distinct offences.
Scenario

Charge

Person stands in road blocking traffic, no specific
Garda target

s.9

Person physically blocks a Garda from making an
arrest

s.19(1)

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Scenario

Charge

Protest blocking road

s.9 (consider freedom of assembly considerations — see
ECtHR appendix)

Wilful obstruction + assault on Garda

s.9 + s.19(3) if applicable

Defence Will Attack
Not wilful — medical, accidental, unforeseen. Rebut with detailed observation, BWV, sustained nature.
Lawful authority — e.g., permit, organised event. Rebut by checking authorisation.
Reasonable excuse — medical or other emergency. Treat with care — medical emergency genuine cases should
never be prosecuted under s.9.
Protest — freedom of expression / assembly. Treat protest cases with constitutional / Convention awareness;
engage with senior officer.

Red Flag Escalation
Mass obstruction — multiple persons — consider public meeting / procession framework.
Critical infrastructure blocked (hospital access, emergency services) — escalation may engage other offences.
Protest context — senior officer engagement, recording of the situation in detail, photo / video for the file.

Common Pitfalls
Confusing s.9 (public passage) with s.19(1) (Garda obstruction).
Treating accidental / medical episodes as wilful obstruction.
Inadequate documentation of the obstruction itself — who was passing, what was blocked.
Protest context handled without senior officer or Convention awareness.

Ethics Touchpoints
Standard 6 — impartial application; the same threshold for protest and non-protest obstruction.
Article 10 / 11 ECHR — freedom of expression and assembly in protest contexts; proportionate response.

Compliment Points / Cross-References
Compliment-worthy where Garda navigated a protest scenario with restraint, securing the lawful objective
while protecting rights.
Cross-reference: s.19(1) obstruction (Vol 2); s.20 public meeting / procession offences.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Chapter 3 — Group Disorder Offences
This chapter covers the three escalating group disorder offences: affray (s.16), violent disorder (s.15), and riot
(s.14). These offences share a structural pattern — unlawful violence (use or threat) plus a person of reasonable
firmness apprehending harm — differentiated principally by the number of participants and, for riot, by the
common purpose element.
RGB Mnemonic — Group Disorder Numbers
Riot (s.14) = 12+; Violent Disorder (s.15) = 3+; Affray (s.16) = 2+. The numbers are the differentiator after the
common conduct elements.

3.1 Affray — s.16 CJ(PO)A 1994
Definition

Legal Framework
Statute: s.16 Criminal Justice (Public Order) Act 1994.
Elements: (a) 2+ persons; (b) use or threat of unlawful violence to each other; (c) conduct such as would cause
person of reasonable firmness to fear for safety; (d) any place (public or private — unlike s.5 / s.6, affray is not
restricted to public place).
Person of reasonable firmness: Hypothetical — the test is objective. No actual fearful witness is required,
though their presence strengthens the case.
Court: Indictable / can be tried summarily.
Penalty: Up to 5 years on indictment; 12 months / fine summary.
Power of arrest: s.24 — finds committing.

Track A — Live Incident Response
Officer safety — affray scenes are inherently violent.
Containment — prevent spread; isolate principals.
Identification — each individual must be identifiable for individual charging.
BWV — essential; captures the dynamic; supports individual identification.
Threshold judgment — is this affray (2+ fighting) or violent disorder (3+ fighting / threatening) or just s.6
threatening behaviour.

Track B — Post-Incident Investigation
BWV review and clip extraction for each suspect.
CCTV from surrounding premises.
Witness statements — bystanders, premises staff.
Medical evidence — any IP injuries; suspect injuries (own role / counter-claims).
Custody photographs — each suspect.
Forensic where appropriate (weapons, clothing transfer).

Suspect Strategy and Arrest Power
Specimen Arrest Words
“[Name], I am arresting you for the offence of affray contrary to section 16 of the Criminal Justice (Public
Order) Act 1994. You and another or others have used or threatened unlawful violence towards each other in
such a manner as would cause a person of reasonable firmness present to fear for their safety. You are not
obliged to say anything unless you wish to do so but anything you do say will be taken down in writing and
may be given in evidence.”

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Notebook
Time and place.
Number of persons involved (named where possible).
Specific conduct of EACH person — separation matters for individual charging.
Audience / bystanders — affected persons.
Verbal interventions before arrest.

Interview Plan
Cover the suspect’s individual role — what they personally did; whether others were involved; whether they
used or threatened violence; whether they were involved in a mutual exchange.
Anticipate self-defence raised — s.18 NFOAP framework; document the suspect’s account in detail.
Identification of co-suspects — may inform charge structure.

PULSE / File Index
PULSE incident — affray with multiple subjects.
Member statements (multiple if multiple Gardaí at scene).
BWV clips for each member.
CCTV from surrounding premises.
IP / witness statements.
Medical reports.
Custody photographs of each suspect.
Forensic where applicable.

Charge Selection
Scenario

Charge

Two persons fighting, audience
apprehensive

s.16 affray (each charged)

Three+ persons
threatening

s.15 violent disorder

fighting

or

12+ persons, common purpose,
fighting / threatening

s.14 riot

Single person violent towards
others (no mutual)

s.6 threatening behaviour / s.2 / s.3 NFOAP assault

Affray with substantial injury

s.16 + s.3 / s.4 NFOAP assault

Specimen Wording — Statement of Member
“At 23:50 hrs on [date] outside [premises] on [street], a public place, I and my colleague Garda [name] arrived
in response to a call of fighting. On arrival I observed two males identified subsequently as [name 1] and [name
2] engaged in a fist fight. Each was striking the other; both were on their feet. A crowd of approximately 20
bystanders had formed at a distance. I observed several bystanders backing away with apparent alarm. I formed
the view that the conduct of [names] taken together would cause a person of reasonable firmness present to fear
for the safety of themselves or others. At 23:51 hrs my colleague and I separated [name 1] and [name 2] using
verbal commands and minimum physical intervention. Each was arrested for affray contrary to s.16 CJ(PO)A
1994 and cautioned.”

Defence Will Attack
Not affray — single individual aggressor with passive victim (not mutual). Rebut by establishing both
individuals engaged.
Self-defence — one party claims defensive role. Rebut by sequence of events, who started, force used by each,

opportunity to disengage.
Person of reasonable firmness threshold — no actual audience / no apprehension. Rebut by bystanders present,
their behaviour (backing away, calling Gardaí).
Casey / identification — mistaken identity in fast-moving incident. Rebut by BWV / CCTV / custody process.

Red Flag Escalation
Weapons present — weapons offences (Firearms Acts, Offensive Weapons orders); aggravating sentence
considerations.
Serious injury — s.3 / s.4 NFOAP layered charge.
Group fluctuation upward (3+) — violent disorder threshold.
Death — manslaughter (unlawful act / one-punch case).

Common Pitfalls
Treating an asymmetric assault (one aggressor, passive victim) as affray — affray requires mutual use / threat
of violence.
Failing to individualise the evidence for each suspect.
Missing the threshold differentiator between affray (2+) and violent disorder (3+).
Inadequate audience / bystander evidence — the objective person of reasonable firmness test.

Ethics Touchpoints
Standard 4 — force in separating the parties; minimum necessary.
Standard 6 — same charge threshold across demographic, locations.
De-escalation — verbal command first; physical separation only as needed.

Compliment Points / Cross-References
Compliment-worthy where Garda effectively contained a developing affray, identified principals, used
minimum force, and built a clean evidential file for each suspect.
Cross-reference: s.15 violent disorder (3+); s.14 riot (12+); s.3 / s.4 NFOAP (where injury); manslaughter (Vol
2) where death.

3.2 Violent Disorder — s.15 CJ(PO)A 1994
Definition

Legal Framework
Statute: s.15 Criminal Justice (Public Order) Act 1994.
Elements: (a) 3+ persons together; (b) use or threat of unlawful violence; (c) conduct such as would cause
person of reasonable firmness to fear for safety; (d) any place (public or private).
Together: The persons must act together but need not all act simultaneously or in coordination on a common
plan — the joint use / threat of violence is the connecting feature.
Court: Indictable.
Penalty: Up to 10 years on indictment.
Power of arrest: Arrestable offence.

Track A — Live Incident Response
Officer safety paramount — violent disorder typically more serious than affray; backup.
Containment of the group — prevent spread, isolate principals.
Identification of each of 3+ participants — essential for individual charging.
BWV — multiple members’ BWV ideally; capture each individual’s conduct.
Bystander evidence — third parties affected.

Track B — Post-Incident Investigation
Major investigation in serious cases — SIO consideration where injuries serious or numbers large.
BWV review for individualised evidence — each accused’s conduct.
CCTV recovery from all surrounding premises.
Witness statements — IP / bystanders / premises staff / Garda colleagues.
Forensic — weapons, clothing, blood.
Custody photographs of each suspect.
Communications data where appropriate — evidence of pre-arrangement.

Suspect Strategy and Arrest Power
Specimen Arrest Words
“[Name], I am arresting you for the offence of violent disorder contrary to section 15 of the Criminal Justice
(Public Order) Act 1994. You and others were together using or threatening unlawful violence in such a manner
as would cause a person of reasonable firmness present to fear for the safety of themselves or others. You are
not obliged to say anything unless you wish to do so but anything you do say will be taken down in writing and
may be given in evidence.”
Notebook
Number of persons in the group.
Specific conduct of EACH — critical for individual charges.
Together-acting indicators — movement, communications, mutual support.
Audience / bystanders affected.

Interview Plan
Cover suspect’s role specifically — what they personally did, said, the weapon if any, the target.
Together-acting question — who they were with, prior arrangement, plan.
Anticipate joint-enterprise issues — Reilly foresight.
Self-defence as common defence — explore the sequence of events.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

PULSE / File Index
PULSE incident — violent disorder with each subject linked.
Multiple member statements (often a major investigation).
BWV from each member.
CCTV from all relevant premises.
IP / witness statements.
Medical / forensic reports.
Custody photographs.

Charge Selection
Scenario

Charge

3+ persons in a coordinated brawl
with bystander apprehension

s.15 violent disorder (each charged)

2 persons fighting + 1 bystander
shouting threats

s.16 affray (the 2 fighters); s.6 (the shouter)

3+ persons threatening but no
actual violence

s.15 (use OR threat of violence sufficient)

Violent disorder with serious
injury to specific IP

s.15 + s.3 / s.4 NFOAP

Violent disorder with weapon

s.15 + relevant weapons offences (Vol 9 cross-cutting)

Specimen Wording — Statement of Member
“At 02:00 hrs on [date] in [street], a public place, I and my colleagues responded to multiple emergency calls
of a large fight. On arrival I observed a group of approximately 6 males including [names] engaged in a
sustained physical confrontation. [Names 1, 2 and 3] were attacking [names 4, 5 and 6] with kicks and punches.
[Name 1] held a glass bottle as a weapon. Numerous bystanders were retreating from the scene. I formed the
view that the conduct of the named persons taken together was such as would cause a person of reasonable
firmness present to fear for their own safety or the safety of another. With my colleagues I separated the parties
using minimum necessary force and arrested [name 1] for violent disorder contrary to s.15 CJ(PO)A 1994.”

Defence Will Attack
Not 3+ acting together — separate incidents conflated. Rebut with individualised evidence and joint-acting
indicators.
Self-defence — the accused was defending against attack. Rebut with sequence of events, accused’s role in
starting / escalating.
Casey / identification — chaotic scene, mistaken identity. Rebut by BWV / CCTV / member observation.
Person of reasonable firmness threshold — no actual bystander or any reasonable firmness. Rebut by bystander
reactions, premises staff, CCTV showing scene.

Red Flag Escalation
Weapons — firearms / knives / blunt instruments — weapons offences in addition.
Serious injuries — s.4 NFOAP serious harm charges; potentially manslaughter / attempted murder.
Pre-planning indicators — communications, weapon acquisition, social media — conspiracy considerations.
Group size escalation — 12+ engages riot threshold.
Gangland / organised crime context — senior officer engagement, intelligence linkage.

Common Pitfalls
Treating affray (2 fighters) as violent disorder (requires 3+).

Missing the together-acting element — separate fights are separate offences.
Inadequate documentation of person-of-reasonable-firmness threshold.

Ethics Touchpoints
Standard 4 — force in containing and separating; proportionate to threat.
Standard 6 — same threshold across demographics.
Documentation rigour — group cases are highly contested at trial; documentation must withstand it.

Compliment-worthy where Garda contained a major group disorder, secured individual evidence on each
accused, and built a robust prosecution file.
Cross-reference: s.16 affray (lesser); s.14 riot (greater); s.3 / s.4 NFOAP (injury); weapons offences.

3.3 Riot — s.14 CJ(PO)A 1994
Definition

Legal Framework
Statute: s.14 Criminal Justice (Public Order) Act 1994.
Elements: (a) 12+ persons present together; (b) use or threat of unlawful violence; (c) common purpose; (d)
conduct such as would cause person of reasonable firmness to fear for safety; (e) any place.
Common purpose: Distinguishing feature of riot. Need not be a written agreement; can be inferred from
coordinated conduct, communications, shared objective.
Use of unlawful violence: The accused must have personally used unlawful violence (threat alone may not
suffice for riot — see statutory text).
Court: Indictable.
Penalty: Up to 10 years on indictment.
Power of arrest: Arrestable offence.

Track A — Live Incident Response
Major incident framework — Public Order command, support from PSU (Public Order Unit) typically engaged.
Containment of the area — cordons, dispersal, evacuation of non-participants.
Officer safety paramount — substantial backup, equipment as appropriate.
Identification efforts — photo / video documentation; helicopter / drone where available.
Arrests — typically post-event, based on identification evidence.

Track B — Post-Incident Investigation
Major Investigation Team — SIO appointed.
Forensic strategy — evidence retrieval from scene (substantial scene).
CCTV — extensive recovery from all surrounding premises and Garda / Council systems.
Photographic / video evidence — social media, bystander footage, news media.
Identification process — multiple identifications via VIPER; Casey warning will apply at trial.
Communications data — evidence of common purpose, planning.
Wave-based arrests typically following identification.

Suspect Strategy and Arrest Power
Specimen Arrest Words
“[Name], I am arresting you for the offence of riot contrary to section 14 of the Criminal Justice (Public Order)
Act 1994. You were one of 12 or more persons together using unlawful violence for a common purpose in such
a manner as would cause a person of reasonable firmness present to fear for their safety. You are not obliged
to say anything unless you wish to do so but anything you do say will be taken down in writing and may be
given in evidence.”
Notebook / Evidence
Number of persons in the group (12+ threshold).
Specific conduct of THIS accused — personal use of violence.
Common purpose indicators — shared chants, coordinated movement, signage, social media planning.
Audience / bystander apprehension.

Interview Plan
Cover accused’s individual role — personal conduct, weapons, target.
Common purpose — who they were with, the objective, pre-arrangement.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Pre-event evidence — social media, communications, travel to the location.
Post-event conduct — flight, disposal of evidence.
Anticipate denial of common purpose — prosecution case must establish each accused’s participation in the
shared purpose.

PULSE / File Index
Riot files are major. The file index will be extensive:
SIO log.
Multiple member statements.
BWV from many members.
CCTV from many premises.
Photographic / video archive.
Identification packs (per accused).
Custody records for each accused.
Forensic reports.
Communications data analyses.
Witness statements (bystanders, premises staff, council workers, journalists).
Charge sheets for each accused, with individualised case theory.

Charge Selection
Scenario

Charge

12+ persons with common
purpose using unlawful violence

s.14 riot (each charged)

12+ persons but no common
purpose evidenced

s.15 violent disorder (3+ together threshold)

Riot with serious injuries

s.14 + s.3 / s.4 NFOAP per individual victim

Riot with weapons

s.14 + relevant weapons offences

Riot with criminal damage

s.14 + s.2 Criminal Damage Act 1991

Specimen Wording — Statement of Member
“At 22:00 hrs on [date] in [location], a public place, I observed a group of approximately 30 persons gathered
in the vicinity of [premises]. The group was shouting [common chant] and members were observed throwing
rocks at [target], setting fire to [target], and assaulting members of the public. I observed [accused] throwing a
rock at [premises window] which shattered. The group appeared to act in a coordinated manner pursuing the
common purpose of [shared objective]. The conduct of the group taken together caused multiple bystanders to
flee in apparent fear. I formed the view that the elements of riot under s.14 CJ(PO)A 1994 were made out as
against [accused].”

Defence Will Attack
Not 12+ persons — group smaller; conflation with adjacent crowd. Rebut by detailed numerical evidence, photo
/ video.
No common purpose — individuals acting on own impulses. Rebut by chants, signs, social media, planning
evidence.
Accused did not personally use violence — mere presence in vicinity. Rebut by individualised evidence (BWV
/ CCTV / identification).
Casey / identification — chaotic scene, multiple persons in similar clothing. Rebut by VIPER, image quality,
identifying features.
Constitutional / Convention claim — protest rights. Distinguish lawful protest from riot — violence is the
disqualifying feature.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Red Flag Escalation
Death — manslaughter / murder considerations.
Critical infrastructure attack — specific aggravating context.
Terrorism connection — Offences Against the State Act framework.
Ongoing operation — continuing arrests; trial-management considerations.

Common Pitfalls
Charging riot where the numbers do not meet 12+ — violent disorder is the correct charge.
Failing to establish each accused’s personal use of violence — mere presence is not enough.
Inadequate common purpose evidence — the distinguishing feature.
Identification errors — the Casey warning will be a major trial issue; image-quality / identification methodology
must withstand it.

Ethics Touchpoints
Standard 4 — force in riot containment must be proportionate; PSU training and equipment govern.
Standard 6 — impartial application; recognise the distinction between protest and riot but apply the law evenly.
Article 10 / 11 ECHR — protest rights; violence is the disqualifier; engagement with senior command on edge
cases.

Compliment Points / Cross-References
Compliment-worthy at the major commendation level where Garda member showed bravery in containing a
riot, securing identification evidence under pressure, supporting investigation post-event.
Cross-reference: s.15 violent disorder; s.16 affray; manslaughter / murder where death; criminal damage Vol
1; weapons offences.

Chapter 4 — Property-Adjacent Public Order Offences
This chapter covers the public-order offences that interface with property: entering a building with intent to
commit an offence (s.11) and trespass on a building (s.13). These provisions complement the burglary
framework (Volume 1) and address conduct that falls short of burglary or aggravated trespass.

4.1 Entering Building with Intent to Commit an Offence — s.11 CJ(PO)A
1994
Definition

Legal Framework
Statute: s.11 Criminal Justice (Public Order) Act 1994.
Elements: (a) enters building / curtilage / part thereof; (b) as a trespasser; (c) for the purpose of committing an
offence or interfering with property.
Trespasser: Person who enters without lawful authority, consent, or permission of the lawful occupier.
Purpose: Mens rea — specific intent at the time of entry.
Court: Indictable / can be summary.
Penalty: 6 months / fine summary; 5 years on indictment.
Relationship to burglary (s.12 Criminal Justice (Theft and Fraud Offences) Act 2001): Distinct offence.
Burglary requires entry as a trespasser with intent to commit theft, GBH, criminal damage, or unlawful sexual
offence (or commission of such after entry). s.11 covers the broader “any offence” intent.

Track A — Live Incident Response
Establish trespasser status — absence of consent / authority.
Document the entry — method, timing, items carried.
Form view as to purpose — indications from circumstances, equipment carried, conduct observed.
Arrest under s.24 — finds committing.

Track B — Post-Arrest Processing
Forensic recovery from scene where appropriate.
Statements from premises occupier / staff.
CCTV recovery.
Suspect interview — the purpose-of-entry element is key.
PULSE incident, charge sheet.

Suspect Strategy and Arrest Power
Specimen Arrest Words
“[Name], I am arresting you for an offence under section 11 of the Criminal Justice (Public Order) Act 1994.
You entered [premises] as a trespasser for the purpose of committing an offence or interfering with property
situated therein. You are not obliged to say anything ….”
Interview Plan
Cover identity and confirm presence at the premises.
Authority / consent for entry — the trespasser element.
Purpose of entry — what the accused intended to do.
Items / equipment carried — supports inference of purpose.
Conduct after entry — supports inference of purpose.

Scenario

Charge

Entered shop premises as trespasser intending to steal

charge

Entered as trespasser to commit assault / damage

Burglary s.12 if intended offence within s.12 schedule

Entered as trespasser to interfere with property (no
theft, no s.12 schedule offence)

s.11 CJ(PO)A 1994

Entered as trespasser with no specific purpose

s.13 CJ(PO)A 1994 trespass

Defence Will Attack
Not a trespasser — consent / authority. Rebut by lawful occupier statement.
No purpose to commit offence — entered without criminal intent. Rebut by equipment, conduct, circumstantial
evidence.
Not a building / curtilage. Rebut by location description.

Red Flag Escalation
Vulnerable occupants present — elderly, disabled, child.
Violent intent / weapons found — aggravated burglary / aggravated charges.
Sexual offence intent indicators — specialist response.

Common Pitfalls
Charging s.11 where burglary s.12 fits — burglary is the more serious charge where the s.12 schedule offence
intent is present.
Inadequate evidence of purpose — entry alone is trespass (s.13), not s.11.
Confusion over curtilage — the area immediately surrounding a building is within s.11 if the entry is for the
purpose.

Ethics Touchpoints
Standard 4 — force on arrest proportionate to circumstances.
Documentation — the purpose element is highly contested at trial; rigorous documentation needed.

Compliment / Cross-Reference
Cross-reference: Burglary (Vol 1, Ch 2); Aggravated Burglary (Vol 1); s.13 trespass (less serious).

4.2 Trespass on a Building — s.13 CJ(PO)A 1994
Definition

Legal Framework
Statute: s.13 Criminal Justice (Public Order) Act 1994.
Elements: (a) trespass on building or curtilage; (b) without reasonable excuse; (c) in a manner that causes or is
likely to cause fear in another person.
Trespass: Entry / presence without lawful authority or consent.
Fear-causing manner: Conduct objectively likely to cause fear — not merely the fact of trespass.
Court: Summary only.
Penalty: Class C fine.

Track A — Live Incident Response
Establish trespass and absence of reasonable excuse.
Document the fear-causing manner — erratic conduct, time of night, threats, equipment.
Identify the person(s) caused or likely to be caused fear.
Verbal direction may resolve — the s.8 power overlaps.

Specimen Arrest Words
“[Name], I am arresting you for an offence under section 13 of the Criminal Justice (Public Order) Act 1994.
You have, without reasonable excuse, trespassed on [premises] in such a manner as caused or was likely to
cause fear in another person. You are not obliged to say anything ….”

Charge Selection
Scenario

Charge

Trespasser on premises with intent to commit offence

s.11 (more serious)

Trespasser on premises in fear-causing manner, no
specific purpose

s.13

Trespasser in DV / civil-relationship context

Consider DV Act 2018; harassment / coercion; s.13 may
be ancillary

Defence Will Attack
Not a trespasser — consent / authority. Rebut by occupier statement.
Reasonable excuse — emergency, mistaken belief in authority. Address per circumstances.
No fear-causing manner — ordinary entry / passage. Rebut by specific manner of conduct.

Common Pitfalls
Treating mere trespass as s.13 — the fear-causing manner is the threshold.
Civil trespass mishandled as criminal — not every trespass is s.13.

Cross-Reference
Cross-reference: s.11 (more serious); Burglary Vol 1; DV Act 2018 (Vol 4).

Chapter 5 — Specialist Public Order Provisions
This chapter covers the specialist POA offences: blackmail (s.17 — demanding money with menaces), laser
pointer misuse (s.23), crowd control offences (s.22), and public meeting / procession offences (s.20). These are
lower-volume but operationally distinct provisions.

5.1 Blackmail / Demand for Money with Menaces — s.17 CJ(PO)A 1994
Definition

Legal Framework
Statute: s.17 Criminal Justice (Public Order) Act 1994.
Elements: (a) makes a demand; (b) demand is unwarranted; (c) made with menaces; (d) with view to gain or
to cause loss.
Demand: Express or implied; spoken, written, electronic.
Unwarranted: Not made in the belief that the demand is justified and the use of menaces is a proper means.
Menaces: Threats of injury, violence, action detrimental to the IP — not limited to threats of physical violence;
reputational, financial, employment threats may suffice.
Gain / loss: Pecuniary or property gain / loss.
Court: Indictable only.
Penalty: Up to 14 years on indictment.
Arrest power: Arrestable offence — s.4 CJA 1984 detention applies.

Track A — Live Incident Response
Blackmail is rarely live — it typically presents at report stage. Where a live demand is being made (e.g., a
courier collecting cash; an electronic transaction in progress), the response is intelligence-led arrest with
planning.

Track B — Post-Report Investigation
IP statement — full account of demands, communications, history.
Preservation of communications — messages, voicemails, letters, emails.
Identify the demand pattern — single demand or sustained extortion.
Communications data access for unknown demands.
Financial trail — any payments made; bank records.
Where suspect is known — arrest planning, search warrant for premises and devices.

Suspect Strategy and Arrest Power
Specimen Arrest Words
“[Name], I am arresting you on suspicion of blackmail contrary to section 17 of the Criminal Justice (Public
Order) Act 1994. You are alleged to have made an unwarranted demand with menaces against [IP] with a view
to gain. You are not obliged to say anything ….”
Interview Plan
Confirm identity and relationship to IP.
Cover the demand — was it made, when, by what means.
Cover the menace — what was threatened.
Cover the warrant — did the accused believe the demand was justified.
Cover any gain — payment received.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

PULSE / File Index
IP statement(s) — detailed, multi-session if needed.
Communications evidence — messages, voice recordings, transcripts.
Communications data.
Financial records.
Search warrant material from suspect premises / devices.
Custody record, interview transcripts.
Where IP fear / safety concerns — protection / safety planning.

Charge Selection
Scenario

Charge

Demand for money under threat of violence

s.17 blackmail (the principal charge)

Threats to kill / cause serious harm in connection with
demand

s.17 + s.5 NFOAP threats (Vol 2)

Demand for sexual material under threat (sextortion)

s.17 + sexual offences (Vol 6)

Demand was warranted — belief that demand was justified and menaces were a proper means. Rebut by
assessing the suspect’s asserted basis.
No menaces — polite request, civil debt collection. Rebut by tone, content, IP perception.
No demand — misinterpretation. Rebut by communications evidence.
Identification — suspect not the maker. Rebut by communications data, financial trail, surveillance.

Red Flag Escalation
Ongoing safety risk to IP — protection planning.
Organised crime feature — specialist unit engagement; intelligence linkage.
Vulnerable IP — elderly, disabled, isolated.
Multiple IPs same suspect — pattern offending.

Common Pitfalls
Treating civil debt disputes as blackmail — the menaces element is the differentiator.
Failing to preserve communications immediately — IP often deletes messages out of distress.
Inadequate engagement with IP safety — the suspect knows where the IP lives.

Ethics Touchpoints
IP welfare priority — even where prosecution is slow, the IP’s safety is the immediate concern.
Standard 4 — arrest planning proportionate to threat.

5.4 Public Meeting / Procession Offences — s.20 CJ(PO)A 1994
Offences relating to public meetings and processions. The framework intersects with Article 10 / 11 ECHR

Operational Application
Lawful protest is a constitutional and Convention right.

Charging individuals out of a peaceable protest engages careful proportionality assessment.

Counter-protest dynamics.
Sit-in / occupation.
Blocking critical infrastructure (hospitals, emergency routes).
Hate-content protests.

6.1 Begging — Criminal Justice (Public Order) Act 2011
Statutory framework

Operational Application
Verbal direction first — the legislation accommodates a move-on / desist approach.
Charge only where threshold met — intimidating / threatening / aggressive.
Recognise vulnerability — many beggars have housing, addiction, mental health needs. Welfare-side
engagement preferred over criminal disposal where possible.
Aggressive begging crossing into threats / assault — NFOAP charging may be the appropriate route.

Ethics
Standard 4 — proportionate response; verbal engagement preferred.
Welfare and human dignity priority — link to social services where appropriate.
Distinct from criminal charging of those in poverty per se — the offence is the manner, not the act of seeking
alms.

The Intoxicating Liquor Acts (2003, 2008, and predecessors) regulate the sale and consumption of alcohol.
Operationally relevant provisions:
Underage drinking on licensed premises — offences of the licensee, the under-18, the accompanying adult.
Disorderly conduct on licensed premises.

byelaws etc.).

Garda powers re. licensed premises — entry, inspection, intervention.

Operational Engagement
Garda visits to licensed premises — partnership with licensees; early-stage intervention prevents escalation.

6.3 Anti-Social Behaviour Orders — Part 11 Criminal Justice Act 2006
Statutory framework

The ASBO Framework Structure
Operational Application
ASBO process is structured — warning, contract, application — follow the steps; do not skip stages without
good reason.
Document the persistent / significant nature of the conduct.
Engage with families and supports — the framework aims at behaviour change, not just sanction.
Breach of ASBO is a criminal offence — separate prosecution.

Strengths and Limits
Strength — graduated response; warns, then contracts, then orders; preserves the criminal sanction for genuine
non-compliance.
Limit — the framework is sometimes underused; takes time and engagement; not a quick disposal.
Welfare interface — ASBO is appropriate where behaviour intervention is the right response; not where mental
health / addiction is the underlying cause.

This appendix addresses the legal-end practice of public order policing: the burdens of proof framework adapted
to POA offences; the defences likely to be raised; charge selection at the borderlines; file craft for both summary
and indictable POA matters; and trial practice.

Part A — Burdens of Proof in Public Order
A.1 The Framework
Same three-tier structure as Volume 2: legal burden on prosecution to prove every element beyond reasonable
doubt; evidential burden on accused to put any defence in play; legal burden on prosecution to disprove the
defence beyond reasonable doubt. No POA-specific reversed burdens (compare Vol 2 insanity / diminished
responsibility).

A.2 Public Place Threshold — An Element, Not a Defence
Many POA offences require a “public place”. This is a constitutive element — the prosecution must prove it,
not the defence disprove it. Investigation must establish: (a) the precise location; (b) whether the public had
access at the material time; (c) any extension by definition (licensed premises, etc.).

A.3 Mens Rea in POA Offences
Most POA offences require either intent or recklessness. Recklessness is subjective Murray standard (carried
from Vol 2). The investigation should produce evidence of the accused’s awareness of the situation, audience,
effect.

Part B — The Defences in Public Order
B.1 Not a Public Place
Where the offence requires a public place, the defence may argue the location was private. Operationally the
investigator addresses this with: detailed location description in notebook; photograph / map / Garda PEMS
GIS reference; statement of premises owner / licensee where appropriate.

B.2 Lawful Authority / Reasonable Excuse
Several POA offences are subject to a “without lawful authority or reasonable excuse” limb (s.9 obstruction;
s.13 trespass on building). The defence may raise:
Permission from owner / occupier.
Lawful purpose (work, event organiser, lawful protest).
Medical emergency.
Statutory authority (utility worker, contractor with permit).
Prosecution rebuts by establishing the absence of lawful authority through occupier statement, permit checks,
official records.

B.3 Self-Defence (in Group Disorder Cases)
Self-defence under s.18 NFOAP / Dwyer is commonly raised in s.16 affray, s.15 violent disorder, and even
s.14 riot cases. The accused claims they were defending themselves or others. Operationally:
Document who started — the initial aggressor analysis.
Document each party’s opportunity to disengage.
Document the force used by each — was it proportionate to perceived threat.
Excessive self-defence in homicide cases reduces to manslaughter (Dwyer); in non-homicide cases, the defence

either succeeds in full or fails.

B.4 Reasonable Force in Lawful Activity
Where the accused was engaged in lawful activity (e.g., door staff at licensed premises) and used force in the
performance of their function, the defence may rely on the s.18 NFOAP framework. The force must be
reasonable in the circumstances as the accused believed them.

B.5 Freedom of Expression / Assembly (Protest Context)
In protest cases, defences may engage Article 10 / 11 ECHR (and Article 40.6 of the Constitution). The defence
may argue that the conduct (e.g., blocking a road, sitting in a building) was protected expression / assembly.
The prosecution response:
Lawful protest is protected — but restrictions for public order, traffic, others’ rights are permissible if necessary
and proportionate.
Violence is not protected — use or threat of violence in protest disqualifies from Convention protection.
The choice of charge matters — a robust charging decision in a protest context should engage senior officer /
DPP.

B.6 Casey / Identification
In group disorder cases (s.14 / s.15 / s.16), identification of individuals out of a chaotic group is the principal
evidential challenge and the principal defence vector. Operationally:
Multiple Garda BWVs covering the same incident.
VIPER procedure for any contested identification.
ADVOKATE assessment of the sighting conditions.
CCTV from multiple angles.
Custody photographs and physical descriptions taken at arrest.

C.2 Borderline 1 — s.5 / s.6
S.5 (disorderly) and s.6 (threatening) often present together. The differentiator: s.6 requires intent or
recklessness to provoke breach of peace; the conduct is threatening / abusive / insulting in character. S.5 is
offensive conduct — broader, lower threshold. Where threats are central, charge s.6. Where conduct is offensive
but not threatening, s.5.

C.3 Borderline 2 — s.6 / s.5 NFOAP threats (Vol 2)
S.6 CJ(PO)A 1994 (threatening behaviour) and s.5 NFOAP 1997 (threats to kill / cause serious harm) overlap

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

where the words spoken are threats to kill or cause serious harm. NFOAP s.5 is the more serious provision —
10 years on indictment — and is the better charge where the threat content reaches that threshold. POA s.6 is
for the lower-tier threatening / abusive / insulting words.

C.4 Borderline 3 — Affray / Violent Disorder Numbers
Affray = 2+; violent disorder = 3+. In a fluid scene where the numbers fluctuate, the charge depends on the
threshold met by the conduct of the accused and the others actually involved. Where 3+ persons together use
or threaten unlawful violence, violent disorder is the better charge — it captures the group dynamic with the
appropriate seriousness.

C.5 Borderline 4 — Violent Disorder / Riot
Violent disorder (3+) versus riot (12+ with common purpose). The numbers matter, but the common purpose
is the differentiator. A large group without common purpose is violent disorder. A coordinated group with a
shared objective is riot.

C.6 Borderline 5 — Burglary / s.11 Entering Building with Intent
Burglary under s.12 Theft and Fraud Offences Act 2001 is the more serious charge for entry with intent to
commit a schedule offence (theft, GBH, criminal damage, sexual offence). s.11 CJ(PO)A 1994 covers “any
offence or interfering with property” — broader but lower penalty. Where the intended offence is a s.12
schedule offence, burglary is the proper charge.

C.7 Borderline 6 — Blackmail / Robbery / Extortion-style
Blackmail (s.17 CJ(PO)A 1994) typically involves a demand and a menace separated in time or context from
an immediate snatch. Robbery (s.14 Theft and Fraud Offences Act 2001) involves immediate use of force or
threat of force in the course of theft. Tiger robberies typically involve elements of both — charge each offence
supported by the facts.

Part D — File Craft for Public Order
D.1 Summary File (s.4 / s.5 / s.6 / s.8 / s.9 / s.13)
Charge sheet or summons.
Notebook entries (member and any colleague witness).
BWV clip.
Custody record where applicable.
Any CCTV / witness statements.
PULSE incident reference.

D.2 Indictable File (s.11 / s.14 / s.15 / s.16 / s.17)
All summary-level material.
Cover report with structured architecture (per Vol 2 Defences appendix Part D.1).
Each accused individualised — specific evidence for each, including BWV / CCTV / witness identification.
Forensic where applicable (weapons, clothing transfer, blood, DNA).
Communications data where common purpose / pre-arrangement evidence.
Custody record, interview transcripts.
Antecedent statement, victim impact statement.
Disclosure register.

D.3 Group-Disorder Files (s.14 / s.15) — Individualisation
The defining file-craft challenge in group disorder cases is individualisation. Each accused will be defended
separately; each must have a personalised case theory. Operationally:

Each Garda witness statement identifies specific conduct of specific accused.
BWV is clipped per accused.
CCTV is reviewed for each accused’s movements.
Witness statements specify each accused’s role.
The disclosure register is structured per accused as well as per item.

D.4 Protest-Context Files
Protest cases require additional file-craft considerations:
Documentation of senior officer engagement and command decisions.
Photographic / video archive showing the protest character and the policing approach.
Evidence of pre-protest engagement (where applicable) with organisers.
Identification of the specific conduct that crossed from protected protest to criminal offending.
Convention proportionality analysis flagged for prosecution counsel.

Part E — Trial Practice
E.1 District Court Practice
Most POA matters proceed at District Court summarily. The Garda witness:
Reads from notebook (with permission).
Adopts BWV evidence.
Anticipates cross on the public-place threshold, on the request to desist (if applicable), on the lawfulness of the
direction (if applicable).
Maintains factual, neutral demeanour even where the case is contested vigorously.

E.2 Circuit Criminal Court Practice (Indictable POA)
Group disorder and blackmail cases tried at Circuit Court.
Book of Evidence service required.
Pre-trial disclosure exchange.
Individualised case theory tested against each accused.
Casey warnings on identification.
Expert evidence where required (e.g., forensic in group cases).

Appendix — Statutory Anchors (Vol 3 — Public Order)
Precise statutory wording for the principal public order provisions. Sections are gathered for use at file-build,
in cover reports, in court. Verify the live consolidated text on revisedacts.lawreform.ie or irishstatutebook.ie

Part A — Criminal Justice (Public Order) Act 1994 — Definitions and
Powers
A.1 Definitions — s.3
Intoxicated: Means under the intoxicating effect of alcohol, drugs (including hallucinogenic, stimulant,
depressant), solvents, or any combination of these.
Public place: Includes (a) any highway, road, street, bridge, footway, footpath, court, alley, square, passage,
channel, river, harbour, sea, foreshore, beach, dock, quay, wharf; (b) any place to which the public have access
whether as of right or by permission and whether subject to a charge or otherwise; (c) any cemetery, churchyard,
school, college, university, hospital; (d) licensed premises and the immediate environs.

A.2 Arrest Power — s.24
Authorises a member of An Garda Síochána to arrest without warrant any person whom the member finds
committing an offence under specified provisions of the Act — in practice operative for s.4, s.5, s.6, s.7, s.8,
s.9, and most other CJ(PO)A 1994 offences.

A.3 Seizure Power — s.25
Power to seize containers / vessels / property reasonably believed to be used in commission of certain offences
— e.g., open bottles in designated public-drinking restricted areas.

Part B — Foundational POA Offences
B.1 s.4 — Intoxication in Public Place
s.4(1): Any person who is present in a public place while intoxicated to such an extent as would give rise to a
reasonable apprehension that he or she might endanger himself or herself or any other person in his or her
vicinity shall be guilty of an offence.
Penalty: Class E fine.

B.2 s.5 — Disorderly Conduct
s.5(1): It shall be an offence for any person in a public place to engage in offensive conduct (a) between the
hours of midnight and 7 am, or (b) at any time, after having been requested by a member of An Garda Síochána
to desist.
Offensive conduct: Unreasonable behaviour which, having regard to all the circumstances, is likely to cause
serious offence or serious annoyance to any person who is, or might reasonably be expected to be, aware of
such behaviour.
Penalty: Class D fine.

B.3 s.6 — Threatening, Abusive or Insulting Behaviour
s.6(1): It shall be an offence for any person in a public place to use or engage in any threatening, abusive or
insulting words or behaviour with intent to provoke a breach of the peace or being reckless as to whether a
breach of the peace may be occasioned.
Penalty: 3 months / Class C fine.

B.4 s.7 — Distribution / Display of Material

s.7(1): It shall be an offence for any person to distribute or display in any public place any writing, sign or
visible representation which is threatening, abusive, insulting or obscene with intent to provoke a breach of the
peace or being reckless as to whether a breach of the peace may be occasioned.
Penalty: 3 months / Class C fine.

Part C — Direction Offences
C.1 s.8 — Failure to Comply with Direction
Power: Member may direct person whom member reasonably suspects of acting in a manner giving rise to
reasonable apprehension for safety / peace, or committing s.4/5/6/7/8 offence.
Directions: Desist; leave the vicinity; both.
s.8(2): A person who fails to comply with a direction given under this section shall be guilty of an offence.
Penalty: 6 months / Class D fine.

C.2 s.9 — Wilful Obstruction
s.9(1): Any person who, without lawful authority or reasonable excuse, wilfully prevents or interrupts the free
passage of any person or vehicle in any public place shall be guilty of an offence.
Penalty: Class E fine.

Part D — Property-Adjacent POA Offences
D.1 s.11 — Entering Building with Intent
s.11: It shall be an offence for any person to enter any building or the curtilage of any building or any part of
such building or curtilage, as a trespasser, for the purpose of committing an offence or interfering with property
situated therein.
Penalty: 6 months / fine summary; 5 years on indictment.

D.2 s.13 — Trespass on a Building
s.13: It shall be an offence for any person, without reasonable excuse, to trespass on any building or the curtilage
thereof in such a manner as causes or is likely to cause fear in another person.
Penalty: Class C fine.

Part E — Group Disorder Offences
E.1 s.14 — Riot
s.14: Where (a) 12 or more persons who are present together at any place (whether a public or private place or
both) use or threaten to use unlawful violence for a common purpose, and (b) the conduct of those persons,
taken together, is such as would cause a person of reasonable firmness present at that place to fear for his or
her safety, then each of the persons using unlawful violence for the common purpose shall be guilty of the
offence of riot.
Penalty: Up to 10 years on indictment.

E.2 s.15 — Violent Disorder
s.15: Where (a) 3 or more persons who are together use or threaten to use unlawful violence, and (b) the conduct
of those persons, taken together, is such as would cause a person of reasonable firmness present at the place to
fear for his or her own safety or the safety of another, then each of those persons using or threatening to use
unlawful violence shall be guilty of the offence of violent disorder.
Penalty: Up to 10 years on indictment.

E.3 s.16 — Affray
s.16: Where (a) 2 or more persons at any place (whether public or private) use or threaten to use violence
towards each other, and (b) the violence so used or threatened by one of those persons is unlawful, and (c) the
conduct of those persons, taken together, is such as would cause a person of reasonable firmness present at the
place to fear for his or her own safety or the safety of another, then each of the persons using or threatening to
use unlawful violence shall be guilty of the offence of affray.
Penalty: Up to 5 years on indictment; 12 months / fine summary.

Part F — Specialist POA
F.1 s.17 — Blackmail
s.17(1): A person who, with a view to gain for himself or another or with intent to cause loss to another, makes
any unwarranted demand with menaces shall be guilty of the offence of blackmail, extortion and demanding
money with menaces.
Unwarranted: A demand with menaces is unwarranted unless the person making it does so in the belief (a)
that he has reasonable grounds for making the demand and (b) that the use of menaces is a proper means of
reinforcing the demand.
Penalty: Up to 14 years on indictment.

F.2 s.19 — Assault on / Obstruction of Peace Officer
s.19(1): Any person who resists or wilfully obstructs or impedes a peace officer acting in the execution of the
peace officer's duty, or a person assisting a peace officer in the execution of such duty, shall be guilty of an
offence. (Penalty: Class A fine / 6 months summary.)
s.19(3): Any person who assaults or causes bodily harm to a peace officer acting in the execution of the peace
officer's duty, or a person assisting a peace officer in the execution of such duty, shall be guilty of an offence.
(Penalty: Up to 7 years on indictment.)
Note: s.19 is treated in depth in Volume 2 (Person Crime) as it is principally a person-crime offence. The
CJ(PO)A 1994 framework treats it alongside the public order powers for arrest etc.

Part G — Adjacent Legislation
G.1 Criminal Justice Act 2006 — Part 11 (Anti-Social Behaviour Orders)
Statutory framework for Behaviour Warnings (Garda-issued), Good Behaviour Contracts (voluntary), and
ASBOs (court orders on Garda application). Breach of ASBO is a criminal offence.

G.2 Criminal Justice (Public Order) Act 2011 — Begging
Offences of begging in intimidating / aggressive manner; location-specific begging restrictions (near ATMs,
etc.). Replaces historical Vagrancy framework.

G.3 Intoxicating Liquor Acts (2003, 2008)
Framework for the sale, supply, and consumption of alcohol; offences and powers re. licensed premises;
underage drinking; designated drinking places (byelaws).

G.4 Children Act 2001
Juvenile-specific framework including Behaviour Warning, Good Behaviour Contract, Family Conference,
juvenile ASBO. Welfare principle integrated into the framework.

G.5 Article 40.6 of the Constitution — Freedom of Assembly
“The right of the citizens to assemble peaceably and without arms.” Subject to public order and morality.
Engages in protest policing alongside Article 10 / 11 ECHR.

Source verification reminder
Citations below reflect the position at the time of writing. Public order case law continues to develop. Before

Part A - Irish Authority on Mens Rea (Carries to POA Offences)
A.1 The People (DPP) v Murray [1977] IR 360
Citation: [1977] IR 360 (Supreme Court).
Facts: Murray was charged with capital murder of a Garda following a shooting during a bank robbery. The
case turned on the mens rea of capital murder under the (then) Criminal Justice Act 1964 - specifically whether
knowledge that the victim was a Garda was required.
Holding: Recklessness is a subjective concept in Irish criminal law. An accused is reckless if he or she was
aware of a risk and proceeded to take it. The standard is not what a reasonable person would have foreseen but
what this accused actually foresaw.
Reasoning: The Supreme Court (Henchy, Griffin and Parke JJ in particular) rejected an objective recklessness
test as inconsistent with the principle that the prosecution must prove the accused's actual state of mind. Henchy
J's analysis is foundational: "a person is reckless if, knowing that there is a risk that an event may result from
his conduct, or that a circumstance may exist, he takes that risk".
Significance: Foundational Irish authority on subjective recklessness. Applies to every offence in the Code,
the NFOAP, the CJ(PO)A 1994 and beyond where recklessness is an alternative mens rea limb.
Operational Application (Public Order): Every Public Order offence that admits of recklessness - principally
s.6 (threatening, abusive or insulting behaviour with intent OR reckless as to breach of peace) and s.7
(distribution / display) - is tested against the Murray subjective standard. Build the file to evidence the accused's
actual awareness: who was present, what was said, what reaction occurred. Recklessness is not satisfied by
showing what a reasonable person would have appreciated.

A.2 The People (DPP) v Cagney & McGrath [2008] 2 IR 111
Citation: [2008] 2 IR 111; [2007] IESC 46 (Supreme Court).
Facts: Two accused convicted of manslaughter following a single-punch death outside a takeaway. Issue on
appeal: trial judge's direction on recklessness in unlawful-act manslaughter.
Holding: The Supreme Court reaffirmed Murray. Recklessness is subjective. The accused must have been
aware of the risk of serious harm and proceeded to take it. The reasonable-person standard is the objective
check against the accused's account, not the test of liability.
Reasoning: Geoghegan J (with Fennelly and Kearns JJ concurring) emphasised that the subjective standard is
the settled Irish position and rejected any drift toward objective recklessness following English Caldwell
developments and subsequent retraction.
Significance: Reaffirmation at the highest level of the Murray subjective standard. The standard is now beyond
doubt in Irish criminal law.
Operational Application (Public Order): Same as Murray - the recklessness limbs of s.6 and s.7 CJ(PO)A
1994 require evidence of actual awareness. Notebook entries, witness statements, BWV capture of audience
reaction, the accused's own words to colleagues - all build the subjective awareness picture.
