Part B - Irish Authority on Public Order Elements
B.1 The Elements of Affray (s.16 CJ(PO)A 1994)
The Irish case law on s.16 affray establishes the following constitutive features (operational rather than full
vLex treatment given the developing jurisprudence; verify specific decisions before citation):
Mutuality: the use or threat of unlawful violence must be towards each other - i.e., between or among the
participants. A one-sided assault by an aggressor on a passive victim is not affray; it is assault under NFOAP.
Numbers: 2 or more persons engaged in the use or threat of unlawful violence.
Person of reasonable firmness: the objective test. The hypothetical bystander of ordinary firmness, neither
unduly timid nor exceptionally brave. No actual fearful witness is required, but their presence corroborates the
threshold.
Any place: distinguishing affray from s.5/s.6 which require a public place. Affray can be committed in a private
place (e.g., a private house party brawl).
Unlawful: the violence used or threatened by at least one of the participants must be unlawful. Lawful selfdefence by one party does not preclude affray against the aggressor.
Practitioner texts of authority on the elements: Charleton, McDermott & Bolger, Criminal Law (2nd ed.);
McGillicuddy, Criminal Procedure in Ireland; the CJ(PO)A 1994 commentary in Walsh, Criminal Procedure.

B.2 The Elements of Violent Disorder (s.15 CJ(PO)A 1994)
3 or more persons together: 'together' connotes joint conduct, not necessarily simultaneous attack or prearranged plan. The persons are acting together in the use or threat of unlawful violence.
Use or threat of unlawful violence: each accused must individually use or threaten unlawful violence. Mere
presence in a crowd that contains violent participants is not enough.
Person of reasonable firmness threshold: same objective test as affray.
Any place: same as affray.
Individualised liability: each accused is liable for their own use or threat of violence. Group association does
not impute liability automatically. Each must be evidenced individually.

B.3 The Elements of Riot (s.14 CJ(PO)A 1994)
12 or more persons: the numerical threshold. Below 12, the offence is violent disorder.
Common purpose: the distinguishing feature of riot. Need not be a formally agreed plan; can be inferred from
coordinated movement, shared chants or signs, communications evidence.
Use of unlawful violence for the common purpose: each accused must use (not merely threaten) unlawful
violence, and that use must be in furtherance of the common purpose.
Person of reasonable firmness threshold: objective test.
Any place: public or private.
Riot prosecutions are rare in Ireland. The principal modern reference points are media-reported convictions
following major incidents rather than reported appellate authority. The doctrine draws on the underlying
common-law concept of riot adapted by statute.

B.4 The Elements of s.6 Threatening, Abusive or Insulting Behaviour
Public place: as per s.3 definition.
Threatening, abusive or insulting: ordinary meaning; assessed objectively in context.
Words or behaviour: each independently sufficient; combined often the case.
Intent to provoke breach of peace OR recklessness: alternative mens rea limbs. Recklessness is Murraysubjective.
Breach of peace: conduct likely to cause harm to person or property, or fear of same. Drawn from common-law
breach-of-peace doctrine adapted to the statutory framework.

B.5 The Elements of s.17 Blackmail
Demand: any communication that conveys a requirement. Express or implied; spoken, written, electronic; need

not specify amount.
Menaces: threats of injury, violence, or action detrimental to the IP. Not limited to threats of physical violence
- reputational threats, employment threats, threats to disclose information ('sextortion') all qualify if of sufficient
gravity to operate on the mind of an ordinary person of normal stability.
Unwarranted: a demand with menaces is unwarranted unless the accused had reasonable grounds for the
demand AND believed the use of menaces was a proper means of reinforcement. Both limbs.
View to gain / cause loss: pecuniary or property gain/loss in money or money's worth.

Part C - Irish Authority on Arrest, Interview, and Evidence
C.1 The People (DPP) v Walsh [1980] IR 294
Citation: [1980] IR 294 (Supreme Court).
Facts: Walsh was arrested on suspicion of a serious crime. The arresting member's grounds were challenged.
Issue on appeal: the standard required for the arresting member's reasonable suspicion.
Holding: The arresting member must have reasonable suspicion, articulable in fact. The suspicion must be the
member's own (not vicarious from a colleague's instruction without further information). The suspicion need
not amount to prima facie evidence but must be more than mere conjecture.
Reasoning: The Supreme Court framed arrest as a significant intrusion on liberty requiring genuine justification
at the time of arrest. The member need not establish the offence at the moment of arrest but must have facts
known to them that ground reasonable suspicion.
Significance: Foundational Irish authority on the threshold for lawful arrest. Applies to every Garda arrest
including all POA arrests under s.24 CJ(PO)A 1994 and in particular to s.8 direction-based arrests where the
underlying direction itself requires reasonable suspicion / apprehension.
Operational Application: Notebook entry at every arrest must capture: what the member observed; what
reasonable suspicion was formed; the connection between the two. For s.8 directions: the grounds for the
direction (the underlying conduct and reasonable apprehension) must precede the direction. For s.24 findscommitting arrests: the specific conduct observed must support the offence elements.

C.2 The People (DPP) v Buck [2002] 2 IR 268
Citation: [2002] 2 IR 268 (Court of Criminal Appeal).
Facts: Buck was charged with murder. The prosecution relied on inferences from his silence in interview under
s.18 CJA 1984. The trial judge's directions and the prerequisites of the inference were challenged.
Holding: The statutory prerequisites for adverse inferences from silence must be strictly observed. The accused
must be cautioned in the prescribed terms, given a reasonable opportunity to consult a solicitor, and the question
put must fall within the statutory scope. Deviation invalidates the inference.
Reasoning: The Court emphasised that inferences from silence are an inroad on the right to silence and must
be tightly bounded by statutory compliance. The warning is not a formality - it is the procedural protection that
justifies the inferential consequence.
Significance: Strict compliance regime for s.18, s.19, s.19A CJA 1984 inferences. Carries to all serious POA
offences (s.14 riot, s.15 violent disorder, s.16 affray, s.17 blackmail) where the inference regime may be
relevant in interview.
Operational Application: Read the prescribed warning in full; record contemporaneously; allow solicitor
consultation if requested; record the suspect's response; only put the inference question within the statutory
scope. Buck-compliance at interview stage is what makes the inference safe at trial.

C.3 The People (AG) v Casey (No.2) [1963] IR 33
Citation: [1963] IR 33 (Supreme Court).
Facts: Casey was convicted on visual identification evidence. The conviction was challenged on the basis that
the jury had not been adequately warned about the dangers of visual identification.
Holding: Where the prosecution case depends substantially on visual identification, the trial judge must give a
specific warning to the jury about the dangers of identification evidence. The warning must alert the jury that
even honest witnesses can be mistaken and that miscarriages of justice have occurred through such mistakes.
Reasoning: The Court (Kingsmill Moore J) drew on common-law experience of identification mistakes leading
to wrongful conviction. The warning is a structural protection at trial against the inherent risk.
Significance: The Casey warning is mandatory where visual identification is substantial. Failure to give it is
grounds for quashing conviction. Particularly relevant in group disorder cases (s.14/s.15/s.16) where multiple
suspects are identified out of a chaotic dynamic scene.
Operational Application: Anticipate the Casey warning at trial. Investigation must support the identification

C.4 DPP v Gormley & White [2014] IESC 17
Citation: [2014] IESC 17 (Supreme Court).
Facts: Two accused had been interviewed before having an opportunity to consult their solicitors. Inculpatory
statements made. Convictions challenged on basis of pre-interview consultation rights.
Holding: A suspect in custody has a right to consult a solicitor before being interviewed. Interview prior to
vindication of that right renders the resulting statement vulnerable to exclusion. The right is a constitutional
right derived from the right to a fair trial.
Reasoning: Clarke J (delivering the principal judgment) traced the right to fair trial under Article 38.1 and the
European Convention. The pre-interview consultation right became a settled constitutional protection.
Significance: Pre-interview consultation is now the norm in Irish practice. Interviews proceeding without
vindication of the right risk exclusion of resulting evidence.
Operational Application: At custody, ensure solicitor request is taken; facilitate consultation before interview
commencement; do not commence interview until consultation has occurred (or has been validly waived);
document the consultation timing and the suspect's acknowledgments.

C.5 DPP v Doyle [2017] IESC 1
Citation: [2017] IESC 1 (Supreme Court).
Facts: Doyle's interview was conducted without solicitor presence in the interview room. Conviction
challenged on basis that the right to legal assistance extended to solicitor presence during interview.
Holding: The Irish constitutional and Convention right to legal assistance does not extend to a free-standing
right to solicitor presence in the interview room itself. However, where requested by a suspect, the presence of
a solicitor in interview should be facilitated.
Reasoning: The Supreme Court distinguished the pre-interview consultation right (Gormley) from any ininterview presence right. The Constitution does not mandate solicitor presence in every interview; but
operational practice has moved toward accommodating presence where requested.
Significance: Confirms the Gormley pre-interview right and clarifies the in-interview position. Operationally,
Garda interview practice has settled on facilitating solicitor presence where the suspect requests.
Operational Application: Where suspect requests solicitor presence in interview, facilitate. Where the suspect
does not request, the interview can proceed without solicitor presence in the room provided the pre-interview
consultation right has been vindicated.

C.6 DPP v JC [2015] IESC 31
Citation: [2015] IESC 31 (Supreme Court).
Facts: JC's home was searched under an invalid warrant (the form of warrant was constitutionally defective
post-Damache). Evidence was found. Issue on appeal: must the evidence be excluded?
Holding: The strict exclusionary rule from People (DPP) v Kenny [1990] is modified. Evidence obtained in
breach of constitutional rights may be admitted where the breach was inadvertent and not the result of conscious
and deliberate disregard or systemic carelessness. The court conducts a balancing exercise.
Reasoning: Clarke J (delivering the majority judgment) framed a 'good faith' or inadvertence exception to the
Kenny rule. Deliberate or systemically careless breaches still attract exclusion; inadvertent and good-faith
breaches may be admitted in the balance of justice.
Significance: The modern exclusionary rule. Procedural rigor remains essential - the JC margin is narrow.
Deliberate or careless breaches will still result in exclusion.
Operational Application: Do not rely on JC. Comply with constitutional and statutory requirements
rigorously. The JC margin is for inadvertent error in a context of generally sound practice, not for negligent or

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

deliberate cutting of corners.

C.7 Damache v DPP [2012] IESC 11
Citation: [2012] IESC 11 (Supreme Court).
Facts: Damache challenged the constitutionality of s.29 Offences against the State Act 1939 which empowered
a superintendent of An Garda Síochána to issue a search warrant in respect of the investigation he was directing.
Argued the warrant should be issued by an independent person.
Holding: S.29 was unconstitutional in that it permitted the issue of search warrants by a Garda directly involved
in the investigation. The constitutional protection of the dwelling required independent authorisation.
Reasoning: Denham CJ emphasised the constitutional significance of the dwelling under Article 40.5 and the
need for independent judicial or quasi-judicial authorisation of intrusion.
Significance: Search warrants for premises must be independently authorised. Statutory frameworks must
comply. Operational impact is across the investigation framework.
Operational Application: Warrant applications go to District Court / independent authority. Statutory
exceptions (e.g., search incidental to arrest, entry in pursuit of arrest) operate within their own framework.
Searches without warrant in POA / dynamic incident contexts (e.g., entry to building during disturbance) require
the statutory basis to be identified and recorded.

Part D - ECtHR Authority on Expression, Assembly, and Force
D.1 Handyside v United Kingdom (1976) 1 EHRR 737
Citation: Application No. 5493/72, judgment 7 December 1976.
Facts: Handyside, publisher of 'The Little Red Schoolbook', was prosecuted under the Obscene Publications
Act. He claimed breach of Article 10 (freedom of expression).
Holding: Freedom of expression under Article 10 is one of the essential foundations of a democratic society.
It applies not only to information or ideas that are favourably received or regarded as inoffensive but also to
those that offend, shock, or disturb the State or any sector of the population. Restrictions must be: prescribed
by law; pursue a legitimate aim; be necessary in a democratic society.
Reasoning: The Court's analysis: 'such are the demands of that pluralism, tolerance and broadmindedness
without which there is no democratic society'. The proportionality assessment is the operative test for
restriction.
Significance: Foundational Article 10 authority. Sets the framework for all subsequent expression-restriction
cases including protest policing.
Operational Application (Public Order): Where Garda action restricts expression - moving on a protester,
arresting for s.6 threatening behaviour where the words were political - the three-stage test applies: prescribed
by law (statutory basis identified); legitimate aim (public order, others' rights); necessary and proportionate
(was the action the minimum response that would secure the aim?).

D.2 Steel and Others v United Kingdom (1998) 28 EHRR 603
Citation: Application No. 24838/94, judgment 23 September 1998.
Facts: Five applicants were arrested in connection with various protests for breach of the peace. Issue: whether
the breach-of-peace arrests complied with Article 5 (right to liberty) and Article 10 (expression) / Article 11
(assembly).
Holding: Breach-of-peace arrest is compatible with Article 5 where the concept is sufficiently certain and
applied with proportionality. The Court found violation in some applicant cases (arrests for handing out leaflets)
and no violation in others (arrests where genuine breach of peace risk).
Reasoning: The Court accepted that 'breach of the peace' as understood in domestic law is sufficiently certain.
But application must be proportionate. Arrest for genuinely peaceable expressive activity engages violation.
Significance: Distinguishes lawful breach-of-peace arrests in genuine disorder situations from arrests that
suppress peaceable expression. Highly relevant to Irish s.6 / s.8 application in protest contexts.
Operational Application: In protest context: arrest under s.6 / s.8 / breach-of-peace framework only where the
conduct genuinely engages public-order risk. Peaceable expression, even where unwelcome to passers-by, is
protected. Document the public-order risk specifically.

D.3 Bukta and Others v Hungary (2007) Application No. 25691/04
Citation: Application No. 25691/04, judgment 17 July 2007.
Facts: Applicants attended a spontaneous demonstration in response to a sudden political event. Police
dispersed the assembly because notification had not been given as required by domestic law.
Holding: Dispersal of a spontaneous peaceable assembly solely for failure to give prior notification breaches
Article 11. Where the assembly is a response to a current event and prior notification was not practicable, the
right to peaceful assembly attaches.
Reasoning: The Court emphasised that strict application of notification requirements to spontaneous assembly
defeats the purpose of the right. Assemblies responding to immediate events must be accommodated where
peaceable.
Significance: Spontaneous assembly protection. Operational implication for Irish public order policing of
unplanned protests.
Operational Application: Where a peaceable spontaneous assembly forms (e.g., in response to a news event),
the response should be facilitative, not dispersal. Engagement with any apparent organisers; route management;

protection of safety; arrest only where individuals cross into criminal conduct.

D.4 Kudrevicius and Others v Lithuania [GC] (2015) Application No. 37553/05
Citation: Application No. 37553/05, Grand Chamber judgment 15 October 2015.
Facts: Farmers protested by blocking major roads for two days. Significant disruption to road users. Convicted
of rioting under domestic law and sentenced to suspended imprisonment. Issue on Article 11.
Holding: Article 11 protects peaceful assembly but the State has a wider margin where the assembly causes
substantial disruption to public life. Conviction and sanction for sustained, deliberate, significantly disruptive
blocking of major roads did not breach Article 11.
Reasoning: The Grand Chamber emphasised the balance between assembly rights and the rights of others
(public order, freedom of movement, economic activity). Significant deliberate disruption falls outside the most
protected core of Article 11.
Significance: Defines the limit of Article 11 protection in disruptive protest contexts. Distinguishes from Bukta
- spontaneity is one factor; substantial disruption is another.
Operational Application: Where protest involves substantial, deliberate disruption to public infrastructure,
the proportionality calculus permits robust enforcement. Each case is fact-specific; engagement first,
enforcement where genuinely necessary.

D.5 Cestaro v Italy (2015) Application No. 6884/11
Citation: Application No. 6884/11, judgment 7 April 2015.
Facts: Applicant was injured by police during the dispersal of a school occupation at the 2001 Genoa G8
summit. Severe injuries inflicted by baton strikes.
Holding: Violation of Article 3 (prohibition of torture, inhuman or degrading treatment). The use of force was
excessive, unnecessary, and constituted ill-treatment. Italy also breached the procedural obligation under
Article 3 to investigate effectively.
Reasoning: The Court found the conduct rose to the threshold of torture given the severity and the lack of
necessity. The investigative response was inadequate. Article 3 imposes both substantive limits on force and
procedural duties of investigation.
Significance: Force at demonstrations engaging Article 3 where excessive. Procedural obligation to investigate
alleged ill-treatment by police.
Operational Application: Force in public order context must be lawful, necessary, proportionate. Where force
is used, contemporaneous documentation (BWV, statements, custody record) supports the lawful-use
justification. Where alleged ill-treatment is raised, prompt and effective investigation is the institutional
obligation.

D.6 Frumkin v Russia (2016) Application No. 74568/12
Citation: Application No. 74568/12, judgment 5 January 2016.
Facts: Applicant attended a major protest in Moscow which descended into clashes between protesters and
police. Mass arrests followed. Issue: state's handling of the assembly and the absence of dialogue with
organisers.
Holding: Violation of Article 11 (assembly). The authorities failed to maintain communication with the
organisers and missed opportunities for dialogue and de-escalation. The state has a positive obligation to
facilitate peaceable assembly including by constructive engagement with organisers.
Reasoning: The Court emphasised that policing of mass assemblies is not merely a passive containment
function. Active engagement, dialogue, accommodation are part of the Article 11 obligation.
Significance: Positive obligation to engage. Failure to dialogue may itself constitute violation.
Operational Application: Garda response to organised protest engages a positive dimension: pre-event
engagement with organisers; ongoing dialogue during the event; accommodation where practicable. Senior
officer command structure should reflect the dialogue dimension.

D.7 Castle v United Kingdom (2017) Application No. 36636/14 (and related kettling

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

cases)
Citation: Application No. 36636/14, decision 30 May 2017 (also Austin v UK 2012 and the principal kettling
line of authority).
Facts: Applicants were contained ('kettled') by police during demonstrations in London. Containment lasted
several hours. Issue: whether containment engaged Article 5 (liberty) and if so whether proportionate.
Holding: Police containment for crowd-control purposes may, depending on circumstances, fall outside the
scope of Article 5 (Austin) or engage it (other cases). Where Article 5 engaged, containment must be necessary,
proportionate, and time-limited.
Reasoning: The Court distinguished containment driven by genuine public-order necessity from arbitrary
detention. The duration, conditions, and necessity of containment are critical.
Significance: Kettling / containment is operationally sensitive. Its use must be justified, proportionate, and
bounded in time.
Operational Application: Containment as a public-order technique requires senior officer authorisation,
contemporaneous documentation of necessity, time-limitation, accommodation of vulnerable persons within
the contained area, and clear command-level review.

Part E - Irish Constitutional Authority
E.1 Article 40.6.1(i) - Freedom of Expression
'The State guarantees liberty for the exercise of the following rights, subject to public order and morality: The
right of the citizens to express freely their convictions and opinions.'
Operative principles:
Freedom of expression is a foundational right but explicitly subject to public order and morality.
Read alongside Article 10 ECHR which is brought into Irish law by the European Convention on Human Rights
Act 2003.
Restrictions on expression must be justified - the Article itself supplies the public-order limitation but
operational application engages the proportionality test.

E.2 Article 40.6.1(ii) - Freedom of Peaceful Assembly
'The right of the citizens to assemble peaceably and without arms.' Subject to laws for the purpose of preventing
or controlling meetings calculated to cause a breach of the peace or to be a danger or nuisance to the general
public.
Operative principles:
Peaceful assembly is constitutionally protected.
'Peaceably and without arms' - violence or armed assembly is not within the protected sphere.
Restriction for public order, public safety, and prevention of nuisance is anticipated by the text itself.
Read alongside Article 11 ECHR via the 2003 Act.

E.3 Murphy v Independent Radio and Television Commission [1999] 1 IR 12
Citation: [1999] 1 IR 12 (Supreme Court).
Facts: Murphy challenged a statutory prohibition on religious broadcasting. Article 40.6.1(i) expression claim
engaged.
Holding: The Supreme Court accepted the statutory restriction as falling within the public-order / morality
qualification. The reasoning sets out the framework for assessing restriction on expression.
Significance: Modern Irish authority on Article 40.6.1(i) and the proportionality of restriction.
Operational Application: Operational application is limited - this case concerns broadcasting law - but the
constitutional framework analysis is referable in public-order contexts where the constitutional dimension is
engaged.

Part F - Sentencing Authority
F.1 The People (DPP) v Tiernan [1988] IR 250
Citation: [1988] IR 250 (Supreme Court).
Facts: Sentence appeal in a serious sexual offence case. The Supreme Court took the opportunity to set out the
structured discretion framework for sentencing.
Holding: Sentencing in Ireland is a matter of structured judicial discretion. Identify the gravity of the offence
on a scale; identify aggravating factors; identify mitigating factors; arrive at a sentence proportionate to the
offence and the offender.
Significance: Foundational sentencing framework. Applies across the criminal law including POA offences.
Operational Application: File preparation should support the sentencing exercise. Aggravating factors
specific to POA offences include: weapons; vulnerable IP; serious injury; pre-planning; persistent / repeat
offending; impact on community; racial or other discriminatory motive.

F.2 The People (DPP) v M [1994] 3 IR 306
Citation: [1994] 3 IR 306 (Supreme Court / CCA).
Facts: Sentence appeal in a serious case. Court of Criminal Appeal elaborated the Tiernan framework.
Holding: Sentencing is offence-and-offender proportionality. The court considers gravity, aggravation,
mitigation, and arrives at a sentence reflective of all three.
Significance: Refines and amplifies the Tiernan structured discretion approach.
Operational Application: Same as Tiernan - file preparation supports the sentencing exercise on both gravity
and offender-specific factors.

F.3 Sentencing in Public Order Cases - General Patterns
Public order sentencing varies widely by offence and circumstances. General patterns:
s.4 / s.5 / s.6 - typically fine or short suspended sentence; aggravation by repeat offending or context.
s.8 / s.9 - typically fine; custody where contemptuous or repeat.
s.11 - aggravation by occupied premises, weapons, vulnerable persons.
s.13 - typically fine; custody where serious circumstances.
s.16 (affray) - District Court summary disposal often by fine / short custody; Circuit Court indictment where
weapons or serious injury.
s.15 (violent disorder) - typically custodial on indictment; range varies widely by individual role, weapons,
injuries.
s.14 (riot) - substantial custodial sentences; aggravation by pre-planning, weapons, scale of disorder.
s.17 (blackmail) - substantial custodial sentences; aggravation by organised crime context, vulnerable IP, threats
carried out.
Specific sentencing authority on POA offences is found in the Court of Appeal (formerly CCA) judgments
database; verify by parties' names and offence on vLex Ireland.

Part G - Key Operational Takeaways from Case Law
G.1 The Recklessness Subjective Standard
From Murray and Cagney & McGrath: build the file to evidence what the accused actually appreciated, not
what a reasonable person would have foreseen. For s.6 / s.7 POA offences, this means audience evidence, the
accused's own words, the immediate context.

G.2 The Arrest Threshold
From Walsh: notebook entries must capture the member's reasonable suspicion / apprehension at the moment
of arrest or direction. The grounds must be articulable, fact-based, and the member's own.

G.3 The Solicitor Rights Framework
From Gormley and Doyle: pre-interview consultation is a constitutional right; in-interview presence facilitated
where requested. Operational practice has settled on these accommodations and they are not optional.

G.4 The Inference Prerequisites
From Buck: strict compliance. Prescribed warning, consultation opportunity, statutory scope, recorded
response. Cutting corners loses the inference at trial.

G.5 The Identification Framework
From Casey: visual identification requires support. VIPER, ADVOKATE, multiple-angle BWV/CCTV,
contemporaneous descriptions. The Casey warning at trial will test all of this.

G.6 The Exclusionary Margin
From JC: the good-faith exception is narrow. Procedural rigor is the default expectation; do not rely on the JC
margin to rescue careless practice.

G.7 The Convention Overlay in Protest Policing
From Handyside / Steel / Bukta / Kudrevicius / Cestaro / Frumkin / Castle: expression and assembly are
protected; restriction must be lawful, necessary, proportionate; positive dialogue obligation; force is constrained
by Article 3; containment is constrained by Article 5. All of this engages where the Garda public-order response
touches genuine expression or assembly.

G.8 The Constitutional Framework
From Article 40.6 read with the ECHR Act 2003: peaceful expression and peaceful assembly are
constitutionally protected with explicit public-order qualification. Operational decisions in protest context
engage this framework directly; senior officer engagement and recording supports the proportionality analysis.

How to Use Volume 4
Volume 4 covers domestic violence policing - the Garda function with the highest stakes for the people we deal
with. The structure adapts the Vol 1-3 14-block per-incident pattern to the relational, sustained, and patternbased nature of DV offending:

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

The DV Operational Framework
Domestic violence is not an event but a pattern. The Two-Track framework from Volumes 1-3 still applies, but
is overlaid with three DV-specific operational dimensions:

Track A - The Live Incident
Officer safety - DV scenes can escalate; backup as appropriate.
IP safety paramount - separate the parties; check for weapons; check for injuries; check for children present.
Power dynamics - the IP may minimise or recant in the suspect's presence; conduct private interview where
possible.
Children - presence, condition, safety; Tusla notification framework.
Documentation - BWV from arrival; injuries photographed; scene photographed; verbatim statements at first
opportunity.
Threshold judgment - what offence (or offences) are made out; protective order needs.

Track B - The Investigation
Statement-taking at private, supportive location - second appointment may be needed.
Medical evidence - examination, photographs over time as bruising develops.
History review - prior incidents, prior orders, prior PULSE records.
Communications evidence - messages, voicemails, social media.
Third-party evidence - GP records, hospital, school (where children involved), neighbours.
File preparation reflecting the relational and sustained nature of the offending.

The DV-Specific Overlay - Pattern, Power, and Protection

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

DV Risk Assessment - The Core Lethality Markers
Every DV incident is assessed for risk. The principal markers (drawn from the DV2 risk assessment and
lethality research):
Strangulation history - the single strongest predictor of intimate-partner homicide; non-fatal strangulation
increases homicide risk by 7-8x.
Threats to kill - explicit, repeated, or accompanied by means (weapons, planning).
Separation - recently separated or attempting to leave - highest risk period.
Escalation - increasing frequency, severity, or unpredictability.
Weapons - access, threats, prior use.
Coercive control - sustained pattern of monitoring, isolation, threats, financial control.
Pregnancy - elevated risk during and immediately after.
Children - presence; abuse toward; threats against.
Stalking - particularly post-separation.
Suicidal ideation by suspect - associated with intimate-partner homicide risk.
Mental health and substance abuse - by suspect and/or IP.
Prior interventions - prior breaches, prior arrests, prior orders, prior contacts.
Strangulation - The Critical Marker
Non-fatal strangulation is the single most predictive marker of subsequent intimate-partner homicide. Always
ask: 'Has there been any choking, strangling, or pressure on your neck?' Document any answer carefully.
Symptoms include hoarse voice, difficulty swallowing, petechiae in eyes, sore throat, marks (which may appear
hours after the assault). Medical examination is appropriate even where no visible injury. Charge selection: s.4
NFOAP serious harm threshold is frequently met.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Garda Code of Ethics - Domestic Violence Application
DV policing engages every Standard of the Code of Ethics. The relationship-based, sustained, and often hidden
nature of the offending makes ethics not a check but an operational discipline. Five Standards have heightened
salience in this volume.

Standard 1 - Duty
The duty to protect life is the foundational obligation. In DV, that duty has a positive dimension: the State has
obligations under Article 2 ECHR (right to life) to take operational steps to protect persons known to be at risk
(Opuz v Turkey line). This is not an abstract principle - it is operational. A live risk indicator on a DV file
engages the State's positive protection obligation.

Standard 4 - Use of Force
Force in DV scenes is sometimes necessary - to separate parties, to effect arrest, to protect the IP. Force must
be lawful, necessary, proportionate. The presence of the IP and often children means force decisions are
scrutinised in particularly sensitive context. Where verbal de-escalation suffices, force is not appropriate.

Standard 5 - Honesty and Integrity
DV investigations frequently involve complex factual contests - allegations, counter-allegations, prior history,
the IP's varying account. The Garda's honesty in recording what was said, what was observed, and what was
done is the foundation of every DV file. Notebook entries must be contemporaneous. BWV must be activated
and preserved. Statement-taking must reflect what the IP said, not what would be operationally convenient.

Standard 6 - Impartiality
DV occurs across all demographics, cultures, sexualities, and economic strata. Standard 6 requires the same
threshold of intervention across the population. Three particular vigilance points:
Same-sex relationships - DV occurs and must be responded to with the same rigor.
Male IPs - DV against men is under-reported and must be taken seriously.
Cultural sensitivity is not cultural exception - DV is not justified or excused by cultural framework.

Standard 7 - Speaking Up
DV scenes can be culturally fraught even within the Garda response. Where a colleague minimises, dismisses,
or fails to take action on a clear DV incident, the duty to speak up applies. Internal channels (Sergeant, DVLO,
supervision) are available.

The Five DV Ethics Trip-Wires

Compliments - DV-Specific Categories
DV compliments often have a dimension beyond the standard categories. Specific compliment-worthy conduct
in DV:
Strong first response - calm separation of parties, effective IP engagement, comprehensive documentation, child
safeguarding referral.
Sustained investigative engagement - building a coercive control case patiently, supporting the IP through
evidence-gathering, securing prosecution.
Multi-agency coordination - Tusla referral, victim service liaison, protective order assistance.
Risk recognition - identifying lethality markers (strangulation, separation, escalation) and acting on them.
Compassion under pressure - DV scenes are emotionally heavy; sustained kindness to the IP and the children
is compliment-worthy and matters.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

The Trauma Dimension
DV scenes carry occupational trauma for Gardai. Cumulative exposure to violence in intimate relationships, to
frightened IPs, to children at risk, has a welfare cost. Welfare-side recognition and support are not optional
supports - they are part of sustained operational capacity. Use Garda welfare services. Speak to supervisors.
Cumulative exposure is a real factor.
